title: SHARKED Act of 2025
plain_summary: The SHARKED Act of 2025 creates a special team to solve problems with sharks taking fish off fishing lines before fishermen can catch them. This team will include fishing experts, scientists, and people from state agencies who will work together for up to seven years. They will study the problem, find ways to reduce conflicts between sharks and fishermen, and create educational materials to help people understand the issue. The team must report their findings to Congress every two years to show what they have learned and what solutions they recommend.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Supporting the Health of Aquatic systems through Research Knowledge and Enhanced Dialogue Act of two thousand twenty five or the SHARKED Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Shark Depredation Task Force and Research Projects
sections.text: The Secretary of Commerce must create a special team called a task force to identify and solve important problems with shark depredation. Shark depredation happens when sharks take fish that people are trying to catch or damage fishing equipment. The Secretary of Commerce will choose people to serve on this task force. The team will include one person from each Regional Fishery Management Council that manages fishing in different parts of the ocean. It will also include one person from each Marine Fisheries Commission, which are groups that help manage ocean fish. The team will have one person from the fish and wildlife agency of each coastal state and one person from the National Marine Fisheries Service. A coastal state is any state that touches the Atlantic Ocean, Pacific Ocean, Arctic Ocean, Gulf of Mexico, or Long Island Sound. This also includes Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands of the United States, Guam, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, and American Samoa. The task force will also include an expert in managing highly migratory species, which are fish that travel long distances. It will have a researcher who knows about shark management and behavior and another researcher who knows about shark ecology, which is how sharks live and interact with their environment. The task force has several jobs to do. It must find ways to improve coordination and communication between people who manage fisheries and people who study sharks to address shark depredation. The team must identify research priorities and funding opportunities for these priorities. This includes figuring out which shark species are involved in interactions with people, doing shark stock assessments to count how many sharks there are, and understanding how sharks become used to humans and cause more interactions between sharks and people. The task force will study how the way people fish and fishing rules may influence shark interactions. It will look into techniques and strategies to reduce harmful interactions between sharks and humans, including developing and using non lethal deterrents that do not kill sharks. The team will research the role of healthy shark populations in the ocean food web and how climate change affects shifting shark populations, their prey, and shark behavior. The task force must develop recommended management strategies to address shark depredation. It must also coordinate the development and distribution of educational materials to help the fishing community minimize shark interactions by changing how people fish and what they expect. No later than two years after this law is passed, and every two years after that until the task force ends, the task force must submit a report to Congress about what it has found. The task force will end no later than seven years after the Secretary of Commerce creates it. This law also changes the Magnuson Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act by adding projects to better understand shark depredation. These projects will identify what causes increases in shark depredation and determine how to best address shark depredation. Nothing in this law changes the authority and responsibility of the Secretary of Commerce in carrying out the Endangered Species Act of nineteen seventy three or the Magnuson Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act.
